```{r ext-recovery-table}
      `T (°C)`         = T_value,
      Truth             = truth_at_T[[p]],
      Median            = q[2],
      Lower             = q[1],
      Upper             = q[3]
    )
  })
})

ext_param_long |>
  tinytable::tt(digits = 3, escape = TRUE)
```

Now we apply the same `extract_tdt()` call as in @sec-joint-derive — there is no separate "T-varying" workflow because `extract_tdt()` already inverts the fitted surface numerically per draw:

```{r extract-tdt-ext}
#| label: extract-tdt-ext
#| echo: true
#| code-fold: show

# Extract TDT quantities from the extended simulation fit.
et_ext <- extract_tdt(wf_ext, target_surv = "relative", t_ref = 60, time_multiplier = 1,
                      ndraws = 1000, lethal = TRUE)
```

@tbl-extract-ext compares the three classical TDT quantities from `extract_tdt()` to the analytical truth derived above.

```{r extract-tdt-ext-table}
#| label: tbl-extract-ext
#| tbl-cap: "Classical TDT quantities from `extract_tdt()` on the T-varying fit, compared against analytical truth. The single-summary $z$ is read directly from the posterior (under the relative threshold $z = -1/\\beta_1$ per draw — the slope of $\\text{mid}(T)$, with no regression); the truth row reports the mean of the analytical local $z(T)$ of the bent absolute-0.5 $\\log_{10}\\text{LT50}(T)$ curve at the assay temperatures. $CT_{max_{1hr}}$ comes from direct inversion of the fitted 4PL at 60 min per draw."

# Format extracted TDT quantities for the extended simulation fit.
tibble::tibble(
  Quantity     = c("z (°C)",
                   "CTmax at 1 hr (°C)",
                   "T_crit (°C)"),
  Truth        = c(round(true_mean_local_z, 3),
                   round(true_CTmax_1hr, 3),
                   NA_real_),  # T_crit truth involves r* integration; see below
  Median       = c(et_ext$z$summary$z_median,
                   et_ext$CTmax$summary$temp_median,
                   et_ext$T_crit$summary$temp_median),
  Lower        = c(et_ext$z$summary$z_lower,
                   et_ext$CTmax$summary$temp_lower,
                   et_ext$T_crit$summary$temp_lower),
  Upper        = c(et_ext$z$summary$z_upper,
                   et_ext$CTmax$summary$temp_upper,
                   et_ext$T_crit$summary$temp_upper)
) |>
  tinytable::tt(digits = 3, escape = TRUE)
```

Both posterior intervals cover their analytical truths. The single-summary $z$ read from the posterior ($-1/\beta_1$ per draw under the relative threshold) is close to the average local sensitivity of the bent curve, and its credible interval covers the truth (truth = `r round(true_mean_local_z, 2)` °C; posterior median `r round(et_ext$z$summary$z_median, 2)` °C, 95% CrI [`r round(et_ext$z$summary$z_lower, 2)`, `r round(et_ext$z$summary$z_upper, 2)`] °C). The bent absolute-threshold local $z(T)$ itself is available with `target_surv = "absolute", z_local = TRUE`. Direct 4PL inversion at the reference duration also recovers $CT_{max_{1hr}}$ (truth `r round(true_CTmax_1hr, 2)` °C; posterior median `r round(et_ext$CTmax$summary$temp_median, 2)` °C, 95% CrI [`r round(et_ext$CTmax$summary$temp_lower, 2)`, `r round(et_ext$CTmax$summary$temp_upper, 2)`] °C), without assuming a constant asymmetry correction in $T$.

The bent $\log_{10}\text{LT50}(T)$ curve itself is the intermediate object both quantities are built from; it lives in `et_ext$lt50_curve` (the output of `derive_tdt_curve()`) and can be plotted directly (@fig-lt50-curve-ext).

::: {#fig-lt50-curve-ext}

```{r lt50-curve-ext-plot}
#| label: plot-lt50-curve-ext
#| fig-width: 6
#| fig-height: 4

# Plot the extended example LT50 curve.
truth_overlay <- {
  Ts <- seq(min(temps) - 2, max(temps) + 2, by = 0.05)
  tibble::tibble(temp = Ts,
                 duration_median = 10 ^ vapply(Ts, true_log10_LT50,
                                               numeric(1)))
}

plot_tdt_curve(et_ext$lt50_curve) +
  ggplot2::geom_line(data = truth_overlay,
                     ggplot2::aes(x = temp, y = duration_median),
                     linetype = "dashed", colour = "black",
                     inherit.aes = FALSE)
```

Posterior $\text{LT50}(T)$ curve (median + 95% CrI, blue) recovered by `derive_tdt_curve()` from the T-varying fit, with the analytical truth overlaid (dashed black). The curve is visibly bent rather than straight in $T$, and the posterior tracks the truth.
:::

We can also drop down to the per-draw level and compute a *local* $z(T)$ at each assay temperature from the same `et_ext$lt50_curve` posterior — a finite difference of $\log_{10}\text{LT50}$ around $T$ per draw, summarised across draws (@fig-local-z-ext).

```{r local-z-ext}
#| label: local-z-ext
#| echo: true
#| code-fold: show

# Derive local z across the temperature range in the extended example.
# Finite-difference local z at each assay T, per posterior draw, derived
# from et_ext$lt50_curve (the same per-draw LT50(T) curve extract_tdt
# already computed for the OLS).
half_step <- 0.5

local_z_summary <- purrr::map_dfr(temps, function(T_at) {
  z_per_draw <- et_ext$lt50_curve$draws |>
    dplyr::group_by(.draw) |>
    dplyr::summarise(
      z_local = -1 / ((stats::approx(temp, log10(duration_out),
                                     xout = T_at + half_step)$y -
                       stats::approx(temp, log10(duration_out),
                                     xout = T_at - half_step)$y) /
                      (2 * half_step)),
      .groups = "drop"
    )
  q <- stats::quantile(z_per_draw$z_local, c(0.025, 0.5, 0.975),
                       na.rm = TRUE, names = FALSE)
  tibble::tibble(T_at           = T_at,
                 z_local_median = q[2],
                 z_local_lower  = q[1],
                 z_local_upper  = q[3])
})

local_z_summary |>
  dplyr::left_join(
    tibble::tibble(T_at = temps, z_local_truth = truth_ext$local_z_true),
    by = "T_at"
  ) |>
  dplyr::select(T_at, z_local_truth, z_local_median,
                z_local_lower, z_local_upper) |>
  dplyr::rename(
    `T (°C)`              = T_at,
    `z local truth (°C)`  = z_local_truth,
    Median                = z_local_median,
    Lower                 = z_local_lower,
    Upper                 = z_local_upper
  ) |>
  tinytable::tt(digits = 3, escape = TRUE)
```

::: {#fig-local-z-ext}

```{r local-z-ext-plot}
#| label: plot-local-z-ext
#| fig-width: 6
#| fig-height: 4

# Plot how local z changes across temperature in the extended example.
truth_curve_z <- {
  Ts <- seq(min(temps), max(temps), by = 0.1)
  tibble::tibble(T_at = Ts,
                 z_local_truth = vapply(Ts, true_local_z, numeric(1)))
}

ggplot2::ggplot(local_z_summary,
                ggplot2::aes(x = T_at, y = z_local_median)) +
  ggplot2::geom_ribbon(ggplot2::aes(ymin = z_local_lower,
                                    ymax = z_local_upper),
                       fill = "#146C7C", alpha = 0.2) +
  ggplot2::geom_line(colour = "#146C7C", linewidth = 1) +
  ggplot2::geom_line(data = truth_curve_z,
                     ggplot2::aes(x = T_at, y = z_local_truth),
                     linetype = "dashed", colour = "black",
                     inherit.aes = FALSE) +
  ggplot2::labs(x = "Assay temperature (°C)",
                y = expression(local~italic(z)~"(°C per 10-fold change in time)")) +
  ggplot2::theme_classic()
```

Local $z(T)$ recovered from the per-draw $\log_{10}\text{LT50}(T)$ curve (blue median + 95% CrI), with the analytical truth overlaid (dashed black). Where the constant-shape simple-case fit would give the same $z$ at every assay temperature, here local $z$ varies with $T$ in lock-step with the bent $\log_{10}\text{LT50}(T)$ curve.
:::

The same `fit_4pl()` and `extract_tdt()` calls recover the bent $\log_{10}\text{LT50}(T)$ curve, average $z$ across the assay grid, and per-draw $CT_{max_{1hr}}$. Under the constant-shape truth in @sec-joint-derive, near-zero slopes on `up`, $\ell$, and $k$ reduce this calculation to the closed-form case.

### Heat injury accumulation and survival under a fluctuating trace

TLS models can translate dynamic field temperatures into predicted heat-injury (HI) accumulation toward a threshold such as $LT_{50}$ [@jorgensen_unifying_2021; @orsted_finding_2022; @arnold_thermal_2025; @noble2026; @rezende_drosophila_mortality_2020]. The joint 4PL posterior propagates uncertainty in $z$ and $CT_{max_{1hr}}$ through the HI integral and uses the fitted surface to predict survival fraction $S_{\text{pred}}(t)$ along the same trajectory (Equation 9 of the manuscript). `predict_heat_injury()` returns both trajectories in one call.

#### A simulated temperature profile across 5 days

To mimic conditions for a moderately heat-tolerant ectotherm, we generate 5 days of hourly temperatures with a sinusoidal diurnal cycle, ranging from 12 °C overnight to a midday peak of 24 °C. We set the net-accumulation threshold $T_c$ to 20 °C, below which HI does not increase [@orsted_finding_2022; @jorgensen_unifying_2021]. The simulated organism has $CT_{max_{1hr}} \approx `r round(true_CTmax, 1)`$ °C and $z \approx `r round(true_z, 1)`$ °C, so the daily peak remains below the 1-hour critical limit and generates gradual sublethal accumulation.

```{r hi-trace}
#| label: hi-trace
#| echo: true
#| code-fold: show

# Create example temperature traces for heat-injury prediction.
hi_trace <- tibble::tibble(
  time = 0:(24 * 5 - 1),
  temp   = 18 + 6 * sin(2 * pi * (0:(24 * 5 - 1) - 6) / 24)   # range 12-24 °C
)
hi_T_c <- 20    # damage threshold (°C)
```

::: {#fig-hi-trace}

```{r hi-trace-plot}
#| label: plot-hi-trace
#| fig-width: 7
#| fig-height: 2.5

# Plot the example temperature traces used for heat-injury prediction.
ggplot2::ggplot(hi_trace, ggplot2::aes(time, temp)) +
  ggplot2::geom_line(colour = "darkred", linewidth = 0.6) +
  ggplot2::geom_hline(yintercept = hi_T_c, linetype = "dashed", colour = "grey40") +
  ggplot2::geom_hline(yintercept = true_CTmax, linetype = "dotted", colour = "grey40") +
  ggplot2::annotate("text", x = max(hi_trace$time), y = hi_T_c, label = "T_c",
                    hjust = 1, vjust = -0.4, size = 3, colour = "grey40") +
  ggplot2::annotate("text", x = max(hi_trace$time), y = true_CTmax,
                    label = "CTmax_1hr",
                    hjust = 1, vjust = -0.4, size = 3, colour = "grey40") +
  ggplot2::ylim(10, 45) +
  ggplot2::labs(x = "Hour", y = "Temperature (°C)") +
  ggplot2::theme_classic()
```

5-day diurnal temperature trace. The dashed line is the damage-accumulation threshold $T_c = 20$ °C; the dotted line is the simulated organism's $CT_{max_{1hr}} \approx `r round(true_CTmax, 1)`$°C — well above the daily peaks, so accumulation is sub-lethal and gradual.
:::

#### Posterior HI and survival via `predict_heat_injury()`

`predict_heat_injury()` takes a temperature trace plus a fitted workflow, propagates the full posterior of $(\ell, \text{up}, k, \beta_0, \beta_1)$ through the HI integral, and returns the median and 95% credible band of cumulative HI and predicted survival at every time point. We supply `T_c` to enforce zero damage accumulation below the threshold (matching Equation 7 of the manuscript). The model time units here are minutes (because `standardize_data()` was called with `duration_unit = "minutes"`), so the trace's `time` is in hours and damage rates are computed accordingly internally.

```{r hi-predict}
#| label: hi-predict
#| echo: true
#| code-fold: show

# Predict heat injury and survival for the example temperature traces.
# The trace's `time` is in hours; `predict_heat_injury()` reconciles it against
# the model's own time unit (minutes here) internally, so we pass `trace_unit`
# rather than rescaling the trace by hand.
hi <- predict_heat_injury(
  trace        = hi_trace,
  workflow     = wf_bb,
  target_surv  = 0.5,
  T_c          = hi_T_c,
  trace_unit   = "hours",
  ndraws       = 500
)
```

::: {#fig-hi-trajectory}

```{r hi-trajectory-plot}
#| label: plot-hi-trajectory
#| fig-width: 7
#| fig-height: 5.5

# Plot heat-injury and survival trajectories for the example traces.
# `plot_heat_injury()` returns the package's canonical two-panel figure:
# cumulative HI on top (with the 100% = one-LT50 reference line) over the
# predicted survival fraction, both with 95% credible bands.
plot_heat_injury(hi)
```

Posterior heat-injury accumulation (top) and predicted survival fraction (bottom) under the 5-day field trace, drawn by `plot_heat_injury()`. Solid lines are posterior medians across 500 draws from `wf_bb`; shaded bands are 95% credible intervals. The dashed line marks the LT50 threshold (HI = 100%). The two trajectories tell the same story: $S_{\text{pred}}(t)$ falls as HI($t$) crosses 100%.
:::

By the end of day 5 the posterior median HI is `r round(tail(hi$summary$hi_median, 1), 0)`% (95% CI: `r round(tail(hi$summary$hi_lower, 1), 0)`% to `r round(tail(hi$summary$hi_upper, 1), 0)`%), and median predicted survival is `r round(tail(hi$summary$surv_median, 1), 2)` (95% CI: `r round(tail(hi$summary$surv_lower, 1), 2)` to `r round(tail(hi$summary$surv_upper, 1), 2)`).

#### Why are the credible bands so wide?

The posterior bands are wide relative to the precision in @tbl-tls-recovery because the rate multiplier, $10^{(T - CT_{max,1hr})/z}$, is exponential. A 1 °C shift in $CT_{max_{1hr}}$ multiplies per-hour HI by $10^{1/z}$, which is approximately `r round(10^(1 / true_z), 2)`-fold for the simulated $z \approx `r round(true_z, 1)`$ °C. Across a 5-day integral, such differences accumulate. A two-stage projection based only on point estimates hides this uncertainty; the joint posterior makes it visible.

### Validation: recovering known doses {#sec-hi-validation}

Before trusting `predict_heat_injury()` on real field data, we validate it against analytical truth on three reference traces with known dosing structure. `make_temperature_scenarios()` builds the traces; `planted_dose_from_trace()` implements the classical HI integral (Equation 7) directly given known `z` and `CTmax_1hr`. Posterior medians from `predict_heat_injury()` should sit near the analytical truth, and the 95% credible intervals should cover it.

```{r hi-validation}
#| label: hi-validation
#| echo: true
#| code-fold: show

# Validate heat-injury predictions against an analytical planted-dose calculation.
# Build validation traces with known planted doses.
val_scens <- make_temperature_scenarios(
  baseline    = 20,
  spike_temp  = 28,
  n_hours     = 96,
  spike_times_single = 24,
  spike_times_multi  = c(24, 48, 72)
)
val_T_c <- 24

# Compute analytical truth from the generating parameters.
planted <- purrr::map(val_scens, planted_dose_from_trace,
                      z = true_z, CTmax_1hr = true_CTmax, T_c = val_T_c)

# Predict the same doses from the fitted beta-binomial workflow.
predicted <- purrr::map(val_scens, function(sc) {
  predict_heat_injury(
    trace       = sc,
    workflow    = wf_bb,
    target_surv = 0.5,
    T_c         = val_T_c,
    trace_unit  = "hours",
    ndraws      = 300
  )
})

# Compare planted and posterior-predicted final heat injury.
val_table <- tibble::tibble(
  Scenario     = c("Flat", "Single spike", "Multi spike"),
  `Planted HI (%)` = c(tail(planted$flat$hi_cumulative,         1),
                        tail(planted$single_spike$hi_cumulative, 1),
                        tail(planted$multi_spike$hi_cumulative,  1)),
  `Posterior median (%)` = c(tail(predicted$flat$summary$hi_median,        1),
                              tail(predicted$single_spike$summary$hi_median, 1),
                              tail(predicted$multi_spike$summary$hi_median,  1)),
  `Lower (%)` = c(tail(predicted$flat$summary$hi_lower,        1),
                  tail(predicted$single_spike$summary$hi_lower, 1),
                  tail(predicted$multi_spike$summary$hi_lower,  1)),
  `Upper (%)` = c(tail(predicted$flat$summary$hi_upper,        1),
                  tail(predicted$single_spike$summary$hi_upper, 1),
                  tail(predicted$multi_spike$summary$hi_upper,  1))
)
val_table$`Truth in CrI?` <- with(val_table,
                                  `Lower (%)` <= `Planted HI (%)` &
                                  `Planted HI (%)` <= `Upper (%)`)
tinytable::tt(val_table, digits = 2, escape = TRUE)
```

The flat trace stays below $T_c$ for its entire duration and so accrues zero HI, matching the analytical truth exactly. The single- and multi-spike traces deliver known doses analytically; `predict_heat_injury()` recovers each with the truth lying inside its credible interval.

# **Case Study 1: Zebrafish heat tolerance across an oxygen gradient** {#sec-zebrafish-o2}

@saruhashi2026 tested the oxygen- and capacity-limited thermal tolerance (OCLTT) hypothesis — that oxygen availability sets the upper thermal limit — by scoring survival of zebrafish (*Danio rerio*) larvae across assay temperatures (38–40 °C) and exposure durations (7.5–240 min) under different oxygen treatments. We take the diploid larvae and compare **normoxia** with **hyperoxia**, letting both the critical temperature ($CT_{max}$) and the thermal sensitivity ($z$) depend on oxygen inside one joint 4PL. The dual purpose here is methodological *and* a validation: if the joint model is doing its job it should recover the OCLTT prediction the authors report — that added oxygen raises heat tolerance.

## Data and standardisation {#sec-zf-o2-data}

```{r zfo2-load}
#| label: zfo2-load
#| echo: true
#| code-fold: show

# Bundled Saruhashi et al. (2026) survival data; diploid larvae, normoxia vs
# hyperoxia (we drop hypoxia, whose z is weakly identified by these durations).
data(zebrafish_o2)
zf_raw <- zebrafish_o2 |>
  dplyr::filter(ploidy == "diploid", oxygen %in% c("normoxia", "hyperoxia")) |>
  dplyr::mutate(oxygen = droplevels(oxygen))

# Counts -> the bayesTLS modelling schema. `oxygen` is carried through as the moderator.
zf_std <- standardize_data(zf_raw, temp = "temp", duration = "duration_min",
                           n_total = "n_total", n_surv = "n_surv",
                           duration_unit = "minutes")
str(zf_std)
```

## Joint 4PL with oxygen on $CT_{max}$ and $z$ {#sec-zf-o2-fit}

We fit $CT_{max}$ and $z$ directly as functions of `oxygen` using cell-means coding (`~ 0 + oxygen`), so each treatment gets its own coefficient and the asymptotes and steepness are shared. The `CTmax`/`z` priors are coding-aware, so this returns the same per-group estimates as treatment coding (`~ oxygen`) — cell-means simply reads most directly.

```{r zfo2-fit}
#| label: zfo2-fit
#| echo: true
#| cache: false
#| code-fold: show

wf_zf <- fit_4pl(zf_std, ctmax = ~ 0 + oxygen, z = ~ 0 + oxygen, t_ref = 60,
                 control    = list(adapt_delta = 0.95),
                 file_refit = "on_change",
                 file       = file.path(models_dir, "zf_oxygen_normhyper"))
summary(wf_zf)
```

## Per-treatment thermal limits and OCLTT check {#sec-zf-o2-extract}

`tls(by = "oxygen")` derives $z$, $CT_{max_{1hr}}$ and $T_{crit}$ per treatment from the shared posterior, so each quantity carries full uncertainty and $T_{crit}$ pairs $CT_{max}$ and $z$ per draw.

```{r zfo2-tls}
#| label: zfo2-tls
#| echo: true

zf_tls <- tls(wf_zf, by = "oxygen", lethal = TRUE)
zf_tls$summary
```

```{r zfo2-facts}
#| echo: false
.zg <- function(ox, q) zf_tls$summary$median[zf_tls$summary$oxygen == ox &
                                              zf_tls$summary$quantity == q]
zf_ctmax_norm  <- .zg("normoxia",  "CTmax");  zf_ctmax_hyper <- .zg("hyperoxia", "CTmax")
zf_tcrit_norm  <- .zg("normoxia",  "Tcrit");  zf_tcrit_hyper <- .zg("hyperoxia", "Tcrit")
```

The joint fit recovers the OCLTT direction @saruhashi2026 report: hyperoxia raises $CT_{max_{1hr}}$ to `r round(zf_ctmax_hyper, 1)` °C, above the normoxic `r round(zf_ctmax_norm, 1)` °C, and lifts $T_{crit}$ markedly (`r round(zf_tcrit_hyper, 1)` °C vs `r round(zf_tcrit_norm, 1)` °C) — added oxygen buys heat tolerance. Saruhashi et al. report this ordering qualitatively rather than as tabulated $z$/$CT_{max}$, so the validation here is the recovered *direction*, not a number-for-number match. (Normoxia's $z$ is wide — survival saturates over the tested durations — while hyperoxia's is tight; the $CT_{max}$/$T_{crit}$ ordering is the well-identified signal.)

```{r zfo2-survival}
#| label: fig-zfo2-survival
#| fig-cap: "Zebrafish survival surface. Fitted joint-4PL survival probability against exposure duration for normoxia and hyperoxia (posterior median and 95% credible band), with the observed cell proportions overlaid."
#| echo: true
#| code-fold: show

psc_zf <- predict_survival_curves(wf_zf)
plot_survival_curves(psc_zf, observed = zf_std)
```

# **Case Study 2: Cereal aphids — comparing three species** {#sec-aphids}

@li2023 measured survival of three cereal-aphid species — *Metopolophium dirhodum*, *Sitobion avenae* and *Rhopalosiphum padi* — across temperatures and exposure durations, and linked their thermal tolerance landscapes to field abundance. We compare the three species on the heat branch at a single age (6 days), modelling $CT_{max}$ and $z$ as functions of species in one joint 4PL so the species contrasts fall straight out of the posterior. This case study does double duty: it shows a **three-group** comparison, validates the fit against Li et al.'s published estimates, and (below) demonstrates `predict_heat_injury()` across multiple groups under a real field temperature trace.

## Data and standardisation {#sec-aphid-data}

```{r aphid-load}
#| label: aphid-load
#| echo: true
#| code-fold: show

# Bundled Li et al. (2023) survival data; heat branch, 6-day-old aphids.
data(aphid_tdt)
aphid_raw <- aphid_tdt |>
  dplyr::filter(branch == "heat", age == "6") |>
  dplyr::mutate(species = droplevels(species))

aphid_std <- standardize_data(aphid_raw, temp = "temp", duration = "duration_min",
                              n_total = "n_total", n_surv = "n_surv",
                              duration_unit = "minutes")   # keeps `species`
str(aphid_std)
```

## Joint 4PL with species on $CT_{max}$ and $z$ {#sec-aphid-fit}

```{r aphid-fit}
#| label: aphid-fit
#| echo: true
#| cache: false
#| code-fold: show

wf_aphid <- fit_4pl(aphid_std, ctmax = ~ 0 + species, z = ~ 0 + species, t_ref = 60,
                    file_refit = "on_change",
                    file       = file.path(models_dir, "aphid_species"))
summary(wf_aphid)
```

## Per-species thermal limits and validation against Li et al. {#sec-aphid-extract}

```{r aphid-tls}
#| label: aphid-tls
#| echo: true

aphid_tls <- tls(wf_aphid, by = "species", lethal = TRUE)
aphid_tls$summary
```

```{r aphid-facts}
#| echo: false
.ag <- function(sp, q) aphid_tls$summary$median[aphid_tls$summary$species == sp &
                                                aphid_tls$summary$quantity == q]
z_rng     <- range(aphid_tls$summary$median[aphid_tls$summary$quantity == "z"])
ctmax_rng <- range(aphid_tls$summary$median[aphid_tls$summary$quantity == "CTmax"])
md_z   <- .ag("M_dirhodum", "z");  rp_ctmax <- .ag("R_padi", "CTmax")
```

Our per-species estimates fall inside the ranges Li et al. report (their Table 1: $z$ ≈ 3.2–4.7 °C, $CT_{max_{1hr}}$ ≈ 35.2–37.3 °C): here $z$ spans `r round(z_rng[1], 1)`–`r round(z_rng[2], 1)` °C and $CT_{max_{1hr}}$ spans `r round(ctmax_rng[1], 1)`–`r round(ctmax_rng[2], 1)` °C. The joint fit also reproduces their species *ordering* — *M. dirhodum* is the least heat-sensitive (highest $z$, `r round(md_z, 1)` °C) and *R. padi* the most heat-tolerant (highest $CT_{max_{1hr}}$, `r round(rp_ctmax, 1)` °C) — so the model recovers both the magnitudes and the ranking from a single posterior, without per-temperature regressions.

```{r aphid-survival}
#| label: fig-aphid-survival
#| fig-cap: "Aphid survival surfaces. Fitted joint-4PL survival probability against exposure duration for the three species (posterior median and 95% credible band), with the observed cell proportions overlaid."
#| echo: true
#| code-fold: show

psc_aphid <- predict_survival_curves(wf_aphid)
plot_survival_curves(psc_aphid, observed = aphid_std)
```

## Heat injury across species under a field temperature trace {#sec-aphid-hi}

With the per-species TDT surface in hand, `predict_heat_injury()` accumulates damage along an hourly field temperature trace and converts it to predicted survival. We use hourly 2 m air temperature for Wuhan (the hottest of Li et al.'s three Chinese wheat sites) over summer 2016, re-sourced from the Open-Meteo ERA5 archive and shipped with the package (built by `data-raw/fetch_aphid_temp_trace.R`). The trace is used **unmodified** — no warming offset — and `by = "species"` projects all three species at once, demonstrating multi-group heat injury. Li et al. drew their trace from a different weather product and used their own TDT parameters, so this reproduces the *shape* of their Fig. 2 within our posterior framework rather than its exact values.

```{r aphid-hi}
#| label: fig-aphid-hi
#| fig-cap: "Multi-group heat-injury projection. Cumulative heat injury (100% = one LT50 dose) and predicted survival for the three aphid species under the unmodified Wuhan summer-2016 hourly temperature trace, from a single `predict_heat_injury(by = \"species\")` call."
#| echo: true
#| code-fold: show

# Hourly (time, temp) trace per city ships in inst/extdata; take Wuhan.
aphid_trace <- readr::read_csv(
  here::here("inst", "extdata", "data_temp_trace_aphid_summer2016.csv"),
  show_col_types = FALSE) |>
  dplyr::filter(city == "Wuhan") |>
  dplyr::transmute(time = time_h, temp = temp_c)        # hours, degrees C

aphid_hi <- predict_heat_injury(aphid_trace, wf_aphid, target_surv = "relative",
                                trace_unit = "hours", by = "species")
plot_heat_injury(aphid_hi)
```

```{r aphid-hi-facts}
#| echo: false
# End-of-season predicted survival per species (tail of the trace).
.aphid_surv_end <- aphid_hi$summary |>
  dplyr::group_by(species) |>
  dplyr::slice_tail(n = 1) |>
  dplyr::select(species, surv_median, surv_lower, surv_upper)
```

The accumulated injury and end-of-season survival differ across species in line with their thermal limits, and the same single call would map the south-to-north climate gradient (Wuhan hottest, Beijing coolest) onto a corresponding gradient in projected survival — the link Li et al. draw between thermal tolerance and field abundance.

# **Case Study 3: Snow gum leaf PSII — light vs dark recovery** {#sec-leaves}

@arnold_psii_2026 developed a thermal-load-sensitivity (TLS) protocol for photosystem II (PSII) and asked, among other things, whether the light environment a leaf experiences *after* heat exposure changes its apparent heat tolerance. Here we take the snow gum (*Eucalyptus pauciflora*) arm of their Experiment 1: excised leaf sections from six mature trees were heated across a grid of assay temperatures (30–56 °C) and exposure durations (5–120 min), then held either in **darkness** or under **90 min of moderate light** before $F_v/F_m$ was re-measured 16–24 h later. The response — retained PSII function, post-exposure $F_v/F_m$ divided by its pre-exposure value — is a **continuous proportion in $[0, 1]$ with no count denominator**, so the 4PL uses a **Beta** likelihood rather than the beta-binomial of the count case studies. Loss of PSII function is **sublethal**, so we report $z$ and $CT_{max}$ but not $T_{crit}$.

We let both $CT_{max}$ and $z$ depend on the recovery condition inside one joint 4PL — a two-group comparison that is also a validation. @arnold_psii_2026 report that post-heat light *lowers* apparent heat tolerance while leaving thermal sensitivity broadly unchanged, so the joint model should place the Light $CT_{max}$ below the Dark one with similar $z$.

## Data and standardisation {#sec-leaf-data}

```{r leaf-load}
#| label: leaf-load
#| echo: true
#| code-fold: show

# Bundled Arnold et al. (2026) snow gum PSII data (Experiment 1, light vs dark
# recovery). Two raw rows with post/pre Fv/Fm marginally above 1 (measurement
# noise) are already excluded upstream, leaving 394 rows.
data(snowgum_psii)
str(snowgum_psii)

# `proportion =` (not n_total/n_surv) flags a continuous response: standardize_data()
# records response_type = "proportion" and clamps to (0.001, 0.999) for the Beta
# density. `recovery` (Dark/Light) and `plant` (six trees) pass through as the
# moderator and the random-effect grouping.
leaf_std <- standardize_data(snowgum_psii, temp = "Temp", duration = "Time",
                             proportion = "fvfm_prop", duration_unit = "minutes")
str(leaf_std)
```

```{r leaf-zero-fraction}
#| echo: false
# Check how often the leaf PSII endpoint reaches zero retained function.
leaf_n_zero   <- sum(snowgum_psii$fvfm_prop <= 0, na.rm = TRUE)
leaf_n_obs    <- sum(!is.na(snowgum_psii$fvfm_prop))
leaf_pct_zero <- round(100 * leaf_n_zero / leaf_n_obs)
```

**Response model and zeros.** Unlike the count-based case studies, the leaf response is a continuous proportion with no denominator, so we use a Beta likelihood. The Beta density is undefined at exactly 0 or 1, but `r leaf_n_zero` of `r leaf_n_obs` observations (`r leaf_pct_zero`%) are zeros — complete loss of measurable PSII function at the hottest, longest exposures. `standardize_data()` clamps these to 0.001 so a single Beta model covers the whole surface. A zero-inflated Beta that separately modelled complete shutdown would represent these structural zeros more faithfully, at the cost of an extra dose-dependent component.

```{r leaf-summary-table}
#| label: tbl-leaf-summary
#| tbl-cap: "Summary of the snow gum leaf PSII dataset after standardisation. Temperature centre is the grand mean used to mean-centre the temperature covariate."

leaf_summary <- tibble::tibble(
  `n observations`            = nrow(leaf_std),
  `n trees (plant)`           = nlevels(leaf_std$plant),
  `Recovery conditions`       = paste(levels(leaf_std$recovery), collapse = ", "),
  `Temperature range (°C)`    = paste(range(leaf_std$temp), collapse = "–"),
  `Duration range (min)`      = paste(round(range(leaf_std$duration), 0), collapse = "–"),
  `Temperature centre (°C)`   = round(attr(leaf_std, "tdt_meta")$temp_mean, 1)
)
tinytable::tt(leaf_summary, digits = 2, escape = TRUE)
```

## Joint 4PL with recovery on $CT_{max}$ and $z$ {#sec-leaf-fit}

We fit $CT_{max}$ and $z$ directly as functions of `recovery` using cell-means coding (`~ 0 + recovery`), so each condition gets its own coefficient. A random intercept on `plant` absorbs among-tree variation in the threshold; `meas_day` has only two levels, too few to estimate a variance, so it is omitted. With the shape parameters left to inherit the same per-condition structure, each recovery condition has its own full dose-response surface, coupled through the shared tree random intercept. The `CTmax`/`z` priors are coding-aware, so cell-means returns the same per-group estimates as treatment coding while reading most directly.

```{r leaf-fit}
#| label: leaf-fit
#| echo: true
#| cache: false
#| code-fold: show

# Beta family is auto-picked from response_type; named here for clarity. The 89
# clamped structural zeros sit on the Beta boundary and induce a small number of
# divergent transitions (~1%); adapt_delta is raised to keep them low. The
# posterior is otherwise well-mixed (Rhat <= 1.003, bulk ESS > 2000).
wf_leaf <- fit_4pl(
  leaf_std,
  ctmax  = ~ 0 + recovery + (1 | plant),
  z      = ~ 0 + recovery,
  family = brms::Beta(link = "identity"),
  t_ref  = 60,
  iter = 6000, warmup = 3000, seed = 123,
  control = list(adapt_delta = 0.999, max_treedepth = 15),
  file_refit = "on_change",
  file = file.path(models_dir, "fit_leaf_recovery_4pl")
)
summary(wf_leaf)
```

```{r leaf-diagnostics}
#| label: tbl-leaf-diagnostics
#| tbl-cap: "Sampling diagnostics for the leaf 4PL fit. Healthy targets: Rhat < 1.01, high ESS. A small residual divergence count comes from the clamped structural zeros at the Beta boundary."
#| cache: false

tinytable::tt(diagnose_tdt_fit(wf_leaf), digits = 3, escape = TRUE)
```

## Per-condition thermal limits {#sec-leaf-extract}

`tls(by = "recovery")` derives $z$ and $CT_{max_{1hr}}$ for each condition from the shared posterior, so each quantity carries full uncertainty. Loss of PSII function is sublethal, so `lethal = FALSE` and no $T_{crit}$ is returned.

```{r leaf-tls}
#| label: leaf-tls
#| echo: true

leaf_tls <- tls(wf_leaf, by = "recovery", lethal = FALSE)
leaf_tls$summary
```

```{r leaf-tls-table}
#| label: tbl-leaf-extract
#| tbl-cap: "Per-condition TDT summaries for snow gum leaf PSII (relative midpoint threshold), posterior medians with 95% credible intervals. $z$ is the temperature slope on the midpoint ($z = -1/\\beta_1$); $CT_{max_{1hr}}$ is the temperature at which the retained-function threshold is reached after 1 h."

leaf_tls_tab <- leaf_tls$summary |>
  dplyr::mutate(quantity = dplyr::recode(quantity,
                                         z = "z (°C)", CTmax = "CTmax_1hr (°C)")) |>
  dplyr::transmute(`Recovery` = recovery, Quantity = quantity,
                   Median = round(median, 2),
                   Lower = round(lower, 2), Upper = round(upper, 2))
tinytable::tt(leaf_tls_tab, escape = TRUE)
```

```{r leaf-facts}
#| echo: false
.lg <- function(cond, q) leaf_tls$summary$median[leaf_tls$summary$recovery == cond &
                                                 leaf_tls$summary$quantity == q]
leaf_ctmax_dark  <- .lg("Dark",  "CTmax"); leaf_ctmax_light <- .lg("Light", "CTmax")
leaf_z_dark      <- .lg("Dark",  "z");     leaf_z_light     <- .lg("Light", "z")
```

The joint fit reproduces the direction @arnold_psii_2026 report: 90 min of post-heat light lowers $CT_{max_{1hr}}$ to `r round(leaf_ctmax_light, 1)` °C, below the dark-recovery `r round(leaf_ctmax_dark, 1)` °C, while thermal sensitivity is essentially unchanged ($z$ ≈ `r round(leaf_z_light, 1)` °C under light vs `r round(leaf_z_dark, 1)` °C in the dark). Post-heat light therefore depresses the apparent tolerance limit without altering how steeply tolerance time falls with temperature — matching their summary that light following heat alters tolerance but not sensitivity. Both quantities fall out of a single posterior, with no per-temperature regressions.

## Retained PSII function curves with observed overlay {#sec-leaf-survival-curves}

`predict_survival_curves()` evaluates the fitted 4PL on a grid of assay temperatures and durations, here returning posterior retained-function proportions for each recovery condition; overlaying the observed proportions checks how closely the fitted surfaces track the data.

```{r leaf-survival-curves}
#| label: fig-leaf-survival-curves
#| fig-cap: "Fitted retained-PSII-function surfaces for snow gum under dark and light post-heat recovery (posterior median and 95% credible band), with observed per-replicate proportions overlaid. The light-recovery curves cross the midpoint at shorter durations, the visual signature of the lower $CT_{max}$."
#| fig-width: 8
#| fig-height: 5
#| echo: true
#| code-fold: show

psc_leaf <- predict_survival_curves(wf_leaf)
plot_survival_curves(psc_leaf, observed = leaf_std) +
  ggplot2::labs(x = "Exposure duration (minutes)",
                y = "Retained PSII function")
```

## Model fit: Bayesian $R^2$ {#sec-leaf-r2}

Loss of PSII function is described by a single joint 4PL (Beta likelihood), so there is one model to report (@tbl-r2-leaf). `bayes_R2_tls()` wraps `brms::bayes_R2()` for a fitted workflow and returns a tidy posterior median with its 95% credible interval.

```{r leaf-r2}
#| label: tbl-r2-leaf
#| tbl-cap: "Bayesian $R^2$ for the snow gum leaf PSII 4PL fit, from `bayes_R2_tls()` (a tidy wrapper around `brms::bayes_R2()`). Posterior median with 95% credible interval."
#| echo: true
#| code-fold: show

bayes_R2_tls(wf_leaf) |>
  dplyr::transmute(
    Model         = "Leaf PSII (4PL, Beta)",
    `Bayesian R²` = format_interval(estimate, lower, upper, digits = 3)
  ) |>
  tinytable::tt(escape = TRUE)
```

# **Case Study 4: Vinegar fly (*Drosophila suzukii*) across sexes**

We'll now turn our attention to a paper by @orsted_suzukii_2024, who measured TDTs for productivity (offspring number; a measure of fertility), time to coma (sublethal) and mortality using *Drosophila suzukii*.

We'll first load the data and start with the mortality data:

#### Load the data
```{r}
#| label: flydat
#| echo: true
#| messages: fales
#| warning: false
#| code-fold: show

# Load the Drosophila data and summarise mortality counts by sex.
data(dsuzukii)

# Count deaths in each temperature-by-time-by-sex group.
mort_dros <- dsuzukii |>
            dplyr::group_by(temp, time, sex) |>
            dplyr::summarise(n_total = dplyr::n(), n_dead = sum(dead), .groups = "drop") |>
            arrange(temp, time, sex)

# Keep sex for the separate and joint models below.

```


#### Fitting the 4PL model
Now that we have the data set up we'll fit the 4PL model:

```{r}
#| label: dros_fit
#| echo: true
#| code-fold: show
#| warning: false

# Fit separate mortality 4PL models for female and male Drosophila.
# Split mortality counts by sex.
mort_dros_F <- dplyr::filter(mort_dros, sex == "F")
mort_dros_M <- dplyr::filter(mort_dros, sex == "M")

# Standardise each sex's counts for the modelling schema.
std_mort_F <- standardize_data(mort_dros_F, temp = "temp", duration = "time",
                               n_total = "n_total", n_dead = "n_dead",
                               duration_unit = "minutes")
std_mort_M <- standardize_data(mort_dros_M, temp = "temp", duration = "time",
                               n_total = "n_total", n_dead = "n_dead",
                               duration_unit = "minutes")

# Fit one 4PL per sex.
wf_dros_mort_F <- fit_4pl(
  std_mort_F,
  chains  = 4, iter = 8000, warmup = 4000, cores = 4, seed = 123,
  control = list(adapt_delta = 0.99, max_treedepth = 12),
  file    = file.path(models_dir, "fit_dsuzukii_mortality_4pl_F")
)

wf_dros_mort_M <- fit_4pl(
  std_mort_M,
  chains  = 4, iter = 8000, warmup = 4000, cores = 4, seed = 123,
  control = list(adapt_delta = 0.99, max_treedepth = 12),
  file    = file.path(models_dir, "fit_dsuzukii_mortality_4pl_M")
)

# Show brms summaries and diagnostics for each fit.
summary(wf_dros_mort_F)
summary(wf_dros_mort_M)

```

The per-sex Bayesian $R^2$ for the two mortality fits:

```{r}
#| label: tbl-r2-dros
#| tbl-cap: "Bayesian $R^2$ for the per-sex *D. suzukii* mortality 4PL fits, via `bayes_R2_tls()` (a tidy wrapper around `brms::bayes_R2()`). Posterior median with 95% credible interval."
#| echo: true
#| code-fold: show

# Format Bayesian R2 for the Drosophila mortality fits.
bayes_r2_table(list(
  Female = wf_dros_mort_F,
  Male   = wf_dros_mort_M
)) |> tinytable::tt(escape = TRUE)
```

Now that we have the models we can extract the TDT for each sex. Note that the models have converged well with base don the Rhat, but we can also look at the MCMC chains if we need do as per below:

```{r}
#| label: dros_chains
#| echo: true
#| include: false
#| eval: false
#| code-fold: show

# Provide optional MCMC trace plots for the Drosophila mortality fits.
plot(wf_dros_mort_M)
plot(wf_dros_mort_F)
```

#### Extracting *z* and $CT_{max_{1hr}}$ for males and females

We can extract the TDT parameters now as we have done in other case studies:

```{r}
#| label: extractTDT
#| echo: true
#| code-fold: show

# Extract z, CTmax and Tcrit from the separate Drosophila mortality fits.
# Use the absolute LT50 threshold and the 4-hour reference exposure.
  tdt_dros_m <- extract_tdt(wf_dros_mort_M, target_surv = "absolute", t_ref = 60*4, lethal = TRUE)
  tdt_dros_f <- extract_tdt(wf_dros_mort_F, target_surv = "absolute", t_ref = 60*4, lethal = TRUE)

# Summarise z.
  z_m <- get_z_summary(tdt_dros_m)
  z_f <- get_z_summary(tdt_dros_f)

# Summarise CTmax at 4 hours.
  ctmax4_m  <- get_ctmax_summary(tdt_dros_m)
  ctmax4_f  <- get_ctmax_summary(tdt_dros_f)

# Summarise Tcrit.
  tcrit_m <- get_tcrit_summary(tdt_dros_m)
  tcrit_f <- get_tcrit_summary(tdt_dros_f)

```

```{r}
#| label: tbl-dros-sex-tdt
#| tbl-cap: "Thermal-death-time parameters for *D. suzukii* mortality, estimated from a separate joint Bayesian 4PL fit per sex (absolute LT~50~ threshold, reference exposure $t_{ref}=4$ h). Cells are the posterior median with the 95% credible interval in brackets. $T_{crit}$ uses the rate-multiplier definition and is available because the endpoint is lethal."
#| message: false
#| warning: false

# Format the Drosophila sex TDT table.
dros_sex_tdt <- data.frame(
  Sex = c("Female", "Male"),
  `z (°C)` = c(
    format_interval(z_f$z_median, z_f$z_lower, z_f$z_upper),
    format_interval(z_m$z_median, z_m$z_lower, z_m$z_upper)
  ),
  `CTmax (4 h, °C)` = c(
    format_interval(ctmax4_f$temp_median, ctmax4_f$temp_lower, ctmax4_f$temp_upper),
    format_interval(ctmax4_m$temp_median, ctmax4_m$temp_lower, ctmax4_m$temp_upper)
  ),
  `T_crit (°C)` = c(
    format_interval(tcrit_f$temp_median, tcrit_f$temp_lower, tcrit_f$temp_upper),
    format_interval(tcrit_m$temp_median, tcrit_m$temp_lower, tcrit_m$temp_upper)
  ),
  check.names = FALSE
)

tinytable::tt(dros_sex_tdt, escape = TRUE)
```

The per-sex TDT parameters are summarised in @tbl-dros-sex-tdt. We see that our *z* values (their Table 1: 3.28 for males, 3.03 for females) as well as $CT_{max_{4hr}}$ (35.2 °C for both sexes) pretty much match @orsted_suzukii_2024 quite well for mortality.

We could ask whether males and females are significantly different or not from each other by simply comparing the posterior distributions. Since we have the full posetrior draws, this is easy to do:

```{r}
#| label: post_comps_dros
#| echo: true
#| code-fold: show
#| message: false
#| warning: false

# Compare female and male posterior z distributions directly.
# Draw z posteriors for each sex.
  z_draws_m  <- get_z_draws(tdt_dros_m)
  z_draws_f  <- get_z_draws(tdt_dros_f)

# Form the female-minus-male contrast.
  diff_sex_z <-  z_draws_f$z - z_draws_m$z   # difference the z columns, not the tibbles

# pMCMC is twice the smaller posterior tail probability.
  pmcmc_sex_z <- 2 * min(mean(diff_sex_z > 0), mean(diff_sex_z < 0))

```

The median difference in *z* between males and females is `r round(median(diff_sex_z), 2)` °C (95% CI: `r round(quantile(diff_sex_z, 0.025), 2)` to `r round(quantile(diff_sex_z, 0.975), 2)` °C; pMCMC = `r round(pmcmc_sex_z, 2)`). The interval spans zero, so the sexes' thermal sensitivities are not clearly distinguishable.

#### Fitting a model to compare sexes directly

We can also fit the sex data in a single model, which is a more powerful approach. To do that, we need to add `sex` as a categorical predictor.

```{r}
#| label: joint_sex_mod
#| echo: true
#| code-fold: show

# Fit a single Drosophila mortality model with sex on the midpoint.
# Prepare one dataset with both sexes and default priors.
std_sex    <- standardize_data(mort_dros, temp = "temp", duration = "time",
                               n_total = "n_total", n_dead = "n_dead",
                               duration_unit = "minutes")
priors_sex <- make_4pl_priors(std_sex)

# Put sex on `mid` so z and CTmax can differ by sex.
joint_sex_fit <- fit_4pl(std_sex, mid = ~ sex * temp_c, prior = priors_sex,
                         t_ref = 60 * 4,
                         chains = 4, iter = 3000, warmup = 1000, cores = 4, seed = 123,
                         control = list(adapt_delta = 0.95),
                         file = file.path(models_dir, "fit_dsuzukii_mortality_sex_joint"),
                         file_refit = "on_change")
summary(joint_sex_fit)
```

We can see here that the interaction `mid_sexM:temp_c` spans zero, supporting little evidence for sex differences in $z$.

We can now do some manual manipulation to show how to recover the same $z$ that `tls()` and the individual per-sex models return. We do this in two steps: **Step 1** computes the *relative* $z$ (the slope of the midpoint line, $-1/\text{slope}$), and **Step 2** computes the *absolute* $z$ (adding the asymmetry correction so the line follows the full-4PL LT$_{50}$). Each printout below is the posterior median bracketed by its 2.5% and 97.5% quantiles — i.e. `2.88 3.06 3.26` means a median of 3.06 with a 95% CI of [2.88, 3.26].

```{r}
#| code-fold: show

# Derive relative and absolute z from the joint Drosophila sex model.
# Extract posterior coefficients from the underlying brms fit.
post <- as_draws_df(get_brmsfit(joint_sex_fit))   # workflow -> underlying brmsfit

# Females are the reference; males add the sex-by-temperature interaction.
slope_F <- post$b_mid_temp_c
slope_M <- post$b_mid_temp_c + post[["b_mid_sexM:temp_c"]]

# Relative z.
# Relative z is the inverse midpoint-temperature slope.
z_F <- -1 / slope_F
z_M <- -1 / slope_M

# Print posterior 2.5%, 50% and 97.5% quantiles.
round(quantile(z_F, c(0.025, 0.5, 0.975)), 2)
round(quantile(z_M, c(0.025, 0.5, 0.975)), 2)

# Absolute z.
# Absolute z follows the corrected full-4PL LT50 curve.
lt50 <- function(tc, slope, intercept) {
  low <- 0.001 + plogis(post$b_lowraw_Intercept + post$b_lowraw_temp_c * tc) * 0.498
  up  <- 0.501 + plogis(post$b_upraw_Intercept  + post$b_upraw_temp_c  * tc) * 0.498
  k   <- exp(post$b_logk_Intercept + post$b_logk_temp_c * tc)
  (intercept + slope * tc) + log((up - 0.5) / (0.5 - low)) / k   # natural log
}

# Estimate the local absolute slope over a 1 C step at the mean temperature.
int_F <- post$b_mid_Intercept
int_M <- post$b_mid_Intercept + post$b_mid_sexM
z_F_abs <- -1 / (lt50(0.5, slope_F, int_F) - lt50(-0.5, slope_F, int_F))
z_M_abs <- -1 / (lt50(0.5, slope_M, int_M) - lt50(-0.5, slope_M, int_M))

round(quantile(z_F_abs, c(0.025, 0.5, 0.975)), 2)
round(quantile(z_M_abs, c(0.025, 0.5, 0.975)), 2)

```

We can also calculate the $CT_{max_{4hr}}$ in similar light:

```{r}
#| code-fold: show
# Derive relative and absolute CTmax from the joint Drosophila sex model.
# Use the 4-hour reference exposure.
t_ref <- 60 * 4
T_bar <- mean(std_sex$temp - std_sex$temp_c)   # centring temperature

# Relative CTmax solves the midpoint line for t_ref.
ctmax_F <- T_bar + (log10(t_ref) - int_F) / slope_F
ctmax_M <- T_bar + (log10(t_ref) - int_M) / slope_M
round(quantile(ctmax_F, c(0.025, 0.5, 0.975)), 2)
round(quantile(ctmax_M, c(0.025, 0.5, 0.975)), 2)

# Absolute CTmax solves the corrected LT50 curve.
slope_abs <- function(slope, intercept) lt50(0.5, slope, intercept) - lt50(-0.5, slope, intercept)
ctmax_F_abs <- T_bar + (log10(t_ref) - lt50(0, slope_F, int_F)) / slope_abs(slope_F, int_F)
ctmax_M_abs <- T_bar + (log10(t_ref) - lt50(0, slope_M, int_M)) / slope_abs(slope_M, int_M)
round(quantile(ctmax_F_abs, c(0.025, 0.5, 0.975)), 2)
round(quantile(ctmax_M_abs, c(0.025, 0.5, 0.975)), 2)
```

Everything above - $z$, $CT_{max_{4hr}}$ and $T_{crit}$, at either the relative or the absolute threshold — can be calculated using a single `tls()` call on any custom `brms` 4PL: it evaluates each sub-parameter at the requested moderator grid with `posterior_linpred()` (just like you would predict with any brms object). Moderators on *any* 4PL parameter (and random effects) are handled automatically. We show both thresholds below.

```{r}
#| label: tls_sex
#| echo: true
#| code-fold: show
# Extract relative TLS summaries by sex from the joint Drosophila model.
set.seed(123)
# One call bundles z, CTmax and T_crit for every level of `by` into a tidy,
# long summary (one row per sex x quantity). target_surv = "relative" uses the
# midpoint (low+up)/2 threshold, matching the Step-1 by-hand calculation.
tls_sex_summary <- tls(joint_sex_fit, by = "sex", target_surv = "relative",
                       lethal = TRUE, t_ref = 60 * 4)$summary

```

```{r}
#| label: tbl-dros-sex-joint
#| tbl-cap: "Thermal-death-time parameters for *D. suzukii* mortality from the single joint 4PL model with sex on the midpoint (`joint_sex_fit`), bundled by one `tls()` call at the **relative** midpoint threshold (`target_surv = \"relative\"`, reference exposure $t_{ref}=4$ h), matching the Step-1 by-hand relative calculation. Cells are the posterior median with the 95% credible interval in brackets. Compare with the separate-fit estimates in the table above."
#| message: false
#| warning: false

# Format the Drosophila sex joint table.
tls_sex_table <- tls_sex_summary |>
  dplyr::transmute(
    Sex      = dplyr::recode(as.character(sex), F = "Female", M = "Male"),
    quantity = factor(quantity,
                      levels = c("z", "CTmax", "Tcrit"),
                      labels = c("z (°C)", "CTmax (4 h, °C)", "T_crit (°C)")),
    cell     = format_interval(median, lower, upper)
  ) |>
  dplyr::arrange(Sex) |>                       # Female before Male, matching the table above
  tidyr::pivot_wider(names_from = quantity, values_from = cell)

tinytable::tt(tls_sex_table, escape = TRUE)
```

Switching to the **absolute** LT~50~ threshold — matching the Step-2 by-hand calculation — needs only `target_surv = "absolute"`:

```{r}
#| label: tls_sex_abs
#| echo: true
#| code-fold: show
# Extract absolute TLS summaries by sex from the joint Drosophila model.
set.seed(123)
# target_surv = "absolute" reads the curve at the 0.5 absolute-survival crossing
# (adding the asymmetry correction), matching the Step-2 by-hand calculation.
tls_sex_summary_abs <- tls(joint_sex_fit, by = "sex", target_surv = "absolute",
                           lethal = TRUE, t_ref = 60 * 4)$summary

```

```{r}
#| label: tbl-dros-sex-joint-abs
#| tbl-cap: "As @tbl-dros-sex-joint but at the **absolute** LT~50~ threshold (`target_surv = \"absolute\"`), matching the Step-2 by-hand absolute calculation. Cells are the posterior median with the 95% credible interval in brackets."
#| message: false
#| warning: false

# Format the Drosophila sex joint absolute table.
tls_sex_table_abs <- tls_sex_summary_abs |>
  dplyr::transmute(
    Sex      = dplyr::recode(as.character(sex), F = "Female", M = "Male"),
    quantity = factor(quantity,
                      levels = c("z", "CTmax", "Tcrit"),
                      labels = c("z (°C)", "CTmax (4 h, °C)", "T_crit (°C)")),
    cell     = format_interval(median, lower, upper)
  ) |>
  dplyr::arrange(Sex) |>
  tidyr::pivot_wider(names_from = quantity, values_from = cell)

tinytable::tt(tls_sex_table_abs, escape = TRUE)
```

The two `tls()` calls reproduce the by-hand calculations: the relative-threshold summary (@tbl-dros-sex-joint) matches the Step-1 relative values ($z$ 3.06 / 3.21, $CT_{max_{4hr}}$ 35.2 / 35.2 °C for female / male), and the absolute-threshold summary (@tbl-dros-sex-joint-abs) matches the Step-2 absolute values ($z$ 3.04 / 3.18, $CT_{max_{4hr}}$ 35.1 / 35.2 °C); $T_{crit}$ is ~29 °C under both.

#### Analysing sublethal measures
The *Drosophila* dataset also includes two sublethal endpoints: heat-coma time and reproductive output.

Heat-coma time can be analysed in two equivalent ways. The classical route fits a Bayesian hierarchical regression of $\log_{10}(\text{time to coma})$ on temperature and derives $z = -1/\beta_1$ and $CT_{max_{1hr}} = \bar{T} + (\log_{10} 60 - \beta_0)/\beta_1$ from the fitted coefficients. The joint route reformulates the same observations as proportion-awake counts and fits the 4PL, recovering the same quantities from the posterior. Below, we fit both models with `sex` as a moderator so one model estimates female- and male-specific parameters.

##### Knockdown: time to coma {#sec-dros-coma}

Each fly was exposed for a fixed duration, scored as awake or in heat coma, and assigned a coma time if it lost the righting response during the exposure. Flies still awake at the end of the exposure had not yet reached the endpoint, so their coma times are **right-censored** at the exposure duration. Overall, `r round(100*mean(is.na(dsuzukii$t_coma)))`% of flies are censored.

Censoring matters because dropping censored flies would select for individuals that entered coma early and potentially bias the $z$ estimate. We retain all flies in two complementary analyses. The **linear** model fits a right-censored log-time regression, treating the exposure duration of censored flies as a lower bound on their unobserved coma time. The **4PL** model instead analyses the binary awake/coma outcome as proportion-awake counts across exposure durations, matching the `awake ~ time` dose-response used by @orsted_suzukii_2024.

```{r}
#| label: dros-coma-data
#| echo: true
#| code-fold: show

# Prepare right-censored Drosophila heat-coma data for modelling.
# Use exposure duration as the right-censoring bound for awake flies.
coma_tbar <- mean(dsuzukii$temp)
coma_dat <- dsuzukii |>
  dplyr::mutate(
    in_coma    = !is.na(t_coma),
    coma_min   = ifelse(in_coma, t_coma, time),  # observed coma time, else exposure time
    log10_coma = log10(coma_min),
    cens       = ifelse(in_coma, "none", "right"),
    temp_c     = temp - coma_tbar,
    # the assay block shared by flies measured together (one temperature x
    # exposure-level x sex group); used as a random intercept below
    block      = interaction(temp, lvl, sex, drop = TRUE)
  )
```

The classical sublethal TDT model is a hierarchical log-time regression, with a censoring term and a `sex` interaction so females and males have separate slopes ($z$) and intercepts ($CT_{max}$). The `(1 | block)` random intercept absorbs variation shared by the flies assayed together in one temperature × exposure-level × sex block, to control for non-independence within a block:

```{r}
#| label: dros-coma-linear-fit
#| echo: true
#| cache: false
#| code-fold: show

# Fit the censored log-time heat-coma model by sex.
priors_coma_lin <- c(
  brms::prior(normal(2, 1.5), class = "Intercept"),
  brms::prior(normal(-1, 1),  class = "b"),
  brms::prior(exponential(2), class = "sd"),
  brms::prior(exponential(2), class = "sigma")
)

fit_coma_lin <- brms::brm(
  brms::bf(log10_coma | cens(cens) ~ temp_c * sex + (1 | block)),
  data       = coma_dat,
  prior      = priors_coma_lin,
  chains     = 4, iter = 6000, warmup = 3000, cores = 4, seed = 123,
  control    = list(adapt_delta = 0.95),
  backend    = "cmdstanr", silent = 2, refresh = 0,
  file       = file.path(models_dir, "fit_dsuzukii_coma_linear"),
  file_refit = "on_change"
)
```

```{r}
#| label: dros-coma-linear-derive
#| echo: true
#| code-fold: show

# Derive z and CTmax from the censored heat-coma model.
# Females are the reference; males add the sex interaction.
post_coma_lin <- posterior::as_draws_df(fit_coma_lin)
slope_F_c <- post_coma_lin$b_temp_c
slope_M_c <- post_coma_lin$b_temp_c + post_coma_lin[["b_temp_c:sexM"]]
int_F_c   <- post_coma_lin$b_Intercept
int_M_c   <- post_coma_lin$b_Intercept + post_coma_lin$b_sexM

z_coma_lin_F  <- -1 / slope_F_c
z_coma_lin_M  <- -1 / slope_M_c
# The fitted model uses temp_c = temp - coma_tbar. The CTmax equation solves
# for temp_c, so add coma_tbar to express the threshold in degrees C.
ct_coma_lin_F <- coma_tbar + (log10(60) - int_F_c) / slope_F_c
ct_coma_lin_M <- coma_tbar + (log10(60) - int_M_c) / slope_M_c
```

The 4PL route reformulates the same data as proportion-awake counts for each temperature-by-exposure-by-sex block. We fit one joint model with `sex` on the midpoint — the same structure as the mortality `joint_sex_fit` above, but here we write the `brms` model out **by hand** (rather than via `fit_4pl(mid = ...)`) to show that `tls()` derives the per-sex quantities from *any* `brms` 4PL, not only from `fit_4pl()` workflows:

```{r}
#| label: dros-coma-4pl-panel
#| echo: true
#| code-fold: show

# Convert heat-coma observations into proportion-awake counts for the 4PL.
coma_panel <- dsuzukii |>
  dplyr::group_by(temp, lvl, sex) |>
  dplyr::summarise(n_total  = dplyr::n(),
                   n_awake  = sum(is.na(t_coma)),   # awake = not (yet) in coma
                   duration = dplyr::first(time),
                   .groups  = "drop") |>
  dplyr::filter(duration > 0)

std_coma        <- standardize_data(coma_panel, temp = "temp", duration = "duration",
                                    n_total = "n_total", n_surv = "n_awake",
                                    duration_unit = "minutes")
priors_coma_4pl <- make_4pl_priors(std_coma)
```

```{r}
#| label: dros-coma-4pl-fit
#| echo: true
#| cache: false
#| code-fold: show

# Build the same sex-on-midpoint 4PL BY HAND with brms::bf() (rather than
# fit_4pl(mid = ...)) to show tls()/extract_tdt() work on ANY brms 4PL.
coma_4pl_bf <- brms::bf(
  n_surv | trials(n_total) ~
    (0.001 + inv_logit(lowraw) * 0.498) +
    ((0.501 + inv_logit(upraw) * 0.498) - (0.001 + inv_logit(lowraw) * 0.498)) /
    (1 + exp(exp(logk) * (logd - mid))),
  lowraw ~ temp_c, upraw ~ temp_c, logk ~ temp_c,
  mid ~ sex * temp_c,                          # sex on midpoint => sex-specific z & CTmax
  nl = TRUE, family = brms::beta_binomial(link = "identity")
)

fit_coma_4pl <- brms::brm(
  coma_4pl_bf, data = std_coma, prior = priors_coma_4pl,
  chains  = 4, iter = 4000, warmup = 2000, cores = 4, seed = 123,
  control = list(adapt_delta = 0.95),
  backend = "cmdstanr", silent = 2, refresh = 0,
  file    = file.path(models_dir, "fit_dsuzukii_coma_4pl"),
  file_refit = "on_change"
)
```

```{r}
#| label: dros-coma-4pl-tls
#| echo: true
#| code-fold: show

# Extract sex-specific heat-coma TLS summaries from the 4PL.
set.seed(123)
# Report z and 1-hour CTmax by sex. fit_coma_4pl is a raw brmsfit (no bayesTLS
# metadata), so pass temp_mean explicitly -- tls() needs the centring temperature.
coma_4pl_tls <- tls(fit_coma_4pl, by = "sex", target_surv = "relative",
                    t_ref = 60, time_multiplier = 1,
                    temp_mean = mean(std_coma$temp - std_coma$temp_c))$summary
```

```{r}
#| label: tbl-dros-coma-compare
#| tbl-cap: "Thermal sensitivity $z$ and the 1-hour critical temperature $CT_{max_{1hr}}$ for *D. suzukii* heat coma, by sex, from the censored linear log-time regression and the joint 4PL on proportion-awake counts, compared with the two-stage drc estimates of @orsted_suzukii_2024 (their static $CT_{max,1h}$). Cells are the posterior median with the 95% credible interval in brackets; the Ørsted values are point estimates."
#| message: false
#| warning: false

# Format the Drosophila coma comparison table.
ctls <- function(q, sx) {
  r <- coma_4pl_tls[coma_4pl_tls$quantity == q & coma_4pl_tls$sex == sx, ]
  format_interval(r$median, r$lower, r$upper)
}
fmt <- function(x) format_interval(stats::median(x),
                                   stats::quantile(x, .025, names = FALSE),
                                   stats::quantile(x, .975, names = FALSE))

coma_compare <- tibble::tribble(
  ~Method,                         ~`z (F)`,            ~`z (M)`,            ~`CTmax 1h (F)`,       ~`CTmax 1h (M)`,
  "Linear (censored log-time)",    fmt(z_coma_lin_F),   fmt(z_coma_lin_M),   fmt(ct_coma_lin_F),    fmt(ct_coma_lin_M),
  "Joint 4PL (proportion awake)",  ctls("z","F"),       ctls("z","M"),       ctls("CTmax","F"),     ctls("CTmax","M"),
  "Ørsted et al. (2024), drc",     "2.23",              "2.33",              "36.36",               "36.31"
)

tinytable::tt(coma_compare, escape = TRUE)
```

Both Bayesian routes agree and recover similar estimates to @orsted_suzukii_2024 within their credible intervals (@tbl-dros-coma-compare). Heat-coma sensitivity is $z \approx 2.3$-2.4 °C and $CT_{max_{1hr}} \approx 36.3$-36.5 °C for both sexes, with little evidence for sex differences.

##### Productivity: reproductive output {#sec-dros-productivity}

For reproductive output, the response has two parts: whether a fly reproduced at all, and how many offspring it produced if it did reproduce. We therefore model productivity with a **hurdle** model rather than a 4PL. The hurdle component estimates the probability of zero offspring, while the positive component models offspring number among reproducing flies. This separates the two biological responses and allows them to vary differently with thermal stress.

As with the 4PL endpoints, productivity changes with exposure duration and temperature. Instead of fitting separate dose-response curves at each temperature and then regressing thresholds, we derive the thermal-death-time quantities directly from the single joint model using the duration and temperature coefficients for each hurdle component.

We compare a Gamma and a log-normal positive part by leave-one-out predictive fit and keep the better one:

```{r}
#| label: dros-prod-data
#| echo: true
#| code-fold: show

# Prepare Drosophila productivity data for the hurdle models.
prod_dat <- dsuzukii |>
  dplyr::mutate(logd = log10(time), temp_c = temp - mean(temp), sex = factor(sex))
prod_tbar <- mean(dsuzukii$temp)
```

```{r}
#| label: dros-prod-fit
#| echo: true
#| cache: false
#| code-fold: show

# Fit and compare hurdle-Gamma and hurdle-lognormal productivity models.
# Allow both hurdle components to vary with exposure, temperature and sex.
prod_form <- brms::bf(prod ~ logd * temp_c * sex,
                      hu   ~ logd * temp_c * sex)
prod_args <- list(formula = prod_form, data = prod_dat,
                  chains = 4, iter = 3000, warmup = 1000, cores = 4, seed = 123,
                  control = list(adapt_delta = 0.95), backend = "cmdstanr",
                  silent = 2, refresh = 0, file_refit = "on_change")

fit_prod_ga <- do.call(brms::brm, c(prod_args,
  list(family = brms::hurdle_gamma(),
       file = file.path(models_dir, "fit_dsuzukii_prod_hurdle_gamma"))))
fit_prod_ln <- do.call(brms::brm, c(prod_args,
  list(family = brms::hurdle_lognormal(),
       file = file.path(models_dir, "fit_dsuzukii_prod_hurdle_lognormal"))))

fit_prod_ga <- brms::add_criterion(fit_prod_ga, "loo")
fit_prod_ln <- brms::add_criterion(fit_prod_ln, "loo")
prod_loo <- brms::loo_compare(fit_prod_ga, fit_prod_ln)
```

```{r}
#| label: dros-prod-loo
#| echo: true
#| code-fold: show

# Compare productivity models by leave-one-out predictive fit.
prod_loo            # Gamma positive part is preferred (elpd difference / SE below)
# Row 2 is the worse model because loo_compare sorts best first.
prod_loo_dz <- abs(prod_loo[2, "elpd_diff"]) / prod_loo[2, "se_diff"]
```

The Gamma positive component predicts the data better than the log-normal by `r round(abs(prod_loo[2,"elpd_diff"]))` expected log predictive-density units (`r round(prod_loo_dz, 1)` SE), so we use `fit_prod_ga` below.

We derive the reproductive summaries directly from the joint posterior.  For reproduction incidence (did you reproduce or not?), this gives threshold durations and their temperature slopes; for conditional clutch size, it gives the duration-sensitivity of positive offspring production. Both are calculated analytically from the fitted coefficients for each posterior draw.

```{r}
#| label: dros-prod-tdt-helper
#| echo: true
#| code-fold: show

# Define helpers for reading productivity TDT summaries from posterior coefficients.
prod_post  <- posterior::as_draws_df(fit_prod_ga)
prod_tbar  <- mean(dsuzukii$temp)
prod_temps <- sort(unique(dsuzukii$temp))
prod_tc    <- prod_temps - prod_tbar

# Extract effective coefficients for each sex and hurdle component.
prod_eff <- function(pre, male) {
  pick <- function(s) { nm <- paste0(pre, s)
    if (nm %in% names(prod_post)) prod_post[[nm]] else 0 }
  list(I  = pick("Intercept")   + (if (male) pick("sexM") else 0),
       T  = pick("temp_c")      + (if (male) pick("temp_c:sexM") else 0),
       D  = pick("logd")        + (if (male) pick("logd:sexM") else 0),
       DT = pick("logd:temp_c") + (if (male) pick("logd:temp_c:sexM") else 0))
}
```

```{r}
#| label: dros-prod-tdt
#| echo: true
#| code-fold: show

# Derive productivity incidence and clutch-size thermal summaries.
# Incidence has a natural 50% threshold, so z and CTmax come from the hurdle part.
inc_tdt <- function(male) {
  cc <- prod_eff("b_hu_", male)
  logd50 <- function(t) -(cc$I + cc$T * t) / (cc$D + cc$DT * t)
  eps <- 1e-3
  zT  <- vapply(prod_tc, function(t)
           -1 / ((logd50(t + eps) - logd50(t - eps)) / (2 * eps)),
           numeric(nrow(prod_post)))
  tcg <- seq(min(prod_tc) - 2, max(prod_tc) + 2, length.out = 400)
  ldm <- vapply(tcg, logd50, numeric(nrow(prod_post)))
  ctm <- apply(ldm, 1, function(r) {
    k <- which(diff(sign(r - log10(60))) != 0)[1]
    if (is.na(k)) NA_real_
    # Add the centring temperature to report CTmax on the observed scale.
    else prod_tbar + stats::approx(r[k:(k + 1)], tcg[k:(k + 1)], log10(60))$y
  })
  list(z = rowMeans(zT), ct = ctm)        # z = mean local z over the assay range
}
prod_inc_F <- inc_tdt(FALSE)
prod_inc_M <- inc_tdt(TRUE)

# Magnitude has no natural LT50, so report duration-sensitivity b(T).
mag_b <- function(male) {
  cc <- prod_eff("b_", male)
  vapply(prod_tc, function(t) (cc$D + cc$DT * t) / log(10), numeric(nrow(prod_post)))
}
mag_b_F <- mag_b(FALSE)
mag_b_M <- mag_b(TRUE)
```

```{r}
#| label: tbl-dros-prod-incidence
#| tbl-cap: "Reproductive **incidence** thermal-death-time parameters for *D. suzukii* (whether a fly reproduces at all; the hurdle component of the hurdle-Gamma model), read analytically from the joint posterior, by sex, with the two-stage drc fecundity estimates of @orsted_suzukii_2024 (static $CT_{max,1h}$) for comparison. $z$ is the mean local $z$ over the assay range. Cells are the posterior median with the 95% credible interval; Ørsted values are point estimates."
#| message: false
#| warning: false

# Format the Drosophila productivity incidence table.
fmt_q <- function(x) format_interval(stats::median(x, na.rm = TRUE),
                                     stats::quantile(x, .025, names = FALSE, na.rm = TRUE),
                                     stats::quantile(x, .975, names = FALSE, na.rm = TRUE))
prod_inc_tbl <- tibble::tribble(
  ~Source,                       ~`z (F)`,            ~`z (M)`,            ~`CTmax 1h (F)`,        ~`CTmax 1h (M)`,
  "Incidence (this study)",      fmt_q(prod_inc_F$z), fmt_q(prod_inc_M$z), fmt_q(prod_inc_F$ct),  fmt_q(prod_inc_M$ct),
  "Ørsted et al. (2024), drc",   "3.27",              "3.46",              "36.27",               "36.66"
)
tinytable::tt(prod_inc_tbl, escape = TRUE)
```

The incidence component gives a conventional reproduction TDT. The estimated $z$ is approximately `r round(stats::median(prod_inc_F$z),1)` °C for females and `r round(stats::median(prod_inc_M$z),1)` °C for males, with $CT_{max_{1hr}}$ from `r round(stats::median(prod_inc_F$ct),1)` to `r round(stats::median(prod_inc_M$ct),1)` °C, close to the fecundity estimates reported by @orsted_suzukii_2024 (@tbl-dros-prod-incidence). This works because reproduction incidence is bounded and has a natural 50% threshold: whether half of flies reproduce. The duration-by-temperature interaction makes the TDT line slightly curved, so we report the mean local $z$ across the assay range.

The magnitude component answers a different biological question. Conditional clutch size is modelled as an unbounded positive response, with no upper asymptote and no natural 50% threshold. Unlike survival or reproduction incidence, there is therefore no single threshold from which to define a conventional $z$. What the joint model does identify is the duration-sensitivity of clutch size, $b(T) = \mathrm{d}\log_{10}(\text{clutch})/\mathrm{d}\log_{10}(\text{exposure})$, which changes strongly with temperature (@tbl-dros-prod-magnitude):

```{r}
#| label: tbl-dros-prod-magnitude
#| tbl-cap: "Duration-sensitivity of the conditional clutch (offspring among flies that reproduced), $b(T) = \\mathrm{d}\\log_{10}(\\text{clutch})/\\mathrm{d}\\log_{10}(\\text{exposure})$, read from the hurdle-Gamma $\\mu$ posterior at each assay temperature, by sex (posterior median). $P(\\text{decline})$ is the posterior probability that $b(T) < 0$. The clutch is insensitive to exposure at 34 °C ($b \\approx 0$) and increasingly reduced by it toward 38 °C."
#| message: false
#| warning: false

# Format the Drosophila productivity magnitude table.
prod_mag_tbl <- tibble::tibble(
  `Temp (°C)`      = prod_temps,
  `b (F)`          = round(apply(mag_b_F, 2, stats::median), 2),
  `P(decline) (F)` = round(apply(mag_b_F, 2, function(b) mean(b < 0)), 2),
  `b (M)`          = round(apply(mag_b_M, 2, stats::median), 2),
  `P(decline) (M)` = round(apply(mag_b_M, 2, function(b) mean(b < 0)), 2)
)
tinytable::tt(prod_mag_tbl, escape = TRUE)
```

Among flies that reproduce, clutch size is essentially unaffected by exposure at 34 °C ($b \approx 0$; $P(\text{decline}) = `r round(mean(mag_b_F[,1] < 0), 2)`$ for females), but declines increasingly steeply at higher temperatures, reaching $b \approx `r round(stats::median(mag_b_F[,5]), 2)`$ at 38 °C.

The hurdle model reveals two effects that a single fecundity curve combines. Heat reduces the probability of reproducing at every tested temperature, giving a clear incidence-based $z \approx 3$ °C. Among flies that still reproduce, however, heat reduces clutch size mainly at higher temperatures ($\gtrsim 36$ °C). Reporting the two components separately makes this biological split visible and provides a more nuanced understanding of temperature/time effects on reproduction.


# **Extended Simulation Results** {#sec-extendsims}

This section provides more detail on the simulation results from the
various scenarios we explored to understand bias and coverage of *z*
and $CT_{max}$. The main manuscript figures summarize the two
shape-perturbing scenarios (drifting upper asymptote `up`, varying slope
`k`) and the bias results from the two partial-mortality sweeps.

Here we document the remaining scenarios we explored: the strict-equivalence
baseline, likelihood misspecification alone, symmetric asymptote
compression, and the design-impact — plus the coverage panels
for the two sweeps reported as bias-only in the main text.

For each scenario we also tabulate the root-mean-square error (RMSE, in °C) of
*z* and $CT_{max,1h}$ — the typical magnitude of the estimation error, combining
bias and variance in a single number on the parameter's own scale — for the four
distinct point estimators. Because RMSE depends only on the point estimates, the
Normal- and *t*-quantile two-stage CI variants share a value and are reported
once each.

```{r sim-supp-setup}
#| label: sim-supp-setup
#| echo: false
#| warning: false
#| message: false

# Load and summarise the full simulation sweep outputs.
library(dplyr)
library(tidyr)
library(ggplot2)
library(here)
library(patchwork)

.method_levels <- c("two_stage_bin", "two_stage_bin_t",
                    "two_stage_bb",  "two_stage_bb_t",
                    "joint_4pl",     "joint_4pl_abs")
.method_labels <- c("Two-stage bin (Normal CI)", "Two-stage bin (t CI)",
                    "Two-stage BB (Normal CI)",  "Two-stage BB (t CI)",
                    "Joint 4PL (relative)",      "Joint 4PL (absolute)")
.method_cols <- setNames(c("#54278f","#cbc9e2","#a63603","#fdae6b",
                           "#006d2c","#a1d99b"), .method_labels)

# Add T_bar after inversion so CTmax is on the original temperature scale.
.TRUTH_REL <- c(z = -1 / -0.15,
                CTmax_1hr = 34 + (log10(60) - 1.5) / -0.15)

.quantity_labeller <- as_labeller(c(
  z          = "z (°C)",
  CTmax_1hr  = "CT[max,1h] (°C)"
))

.theme_proj <- theme_classic(base_size = 11) +
  theme(strip.background = element_blank(),
        strip.text       = element_text(face = "bold"),
        legend.position  = "bottom",
        legend.title     = element_blank(),
        legend.text      = element_text(size = 9),
        panel.spacing    = unit(0.8, "lines"))

.load_screened <- function(prefix) {
  files <- list.files(
    here::here("output", "sim_twostage"),
    pattern = paste0("^per_sim_", prefix, "_.*[.]rds$"),
    full.names = TRUE)
  ps <- dplyr::bind_rows(lapply(files, readRDS))
  bad <- ps |>
    dplyr::filter(method == "two_stage_bb",
                  (quantity == "z"         & abs(bias) > 20) |
                  (quantity == "CTmax_1hr" & abs(bias) > 10)) |>
    dplyr::distinct(scenario, sim_id) |>
    dplyr::mutate(bb_path = TRUE)
  ps |>
    dplyr::left_join(bad, by = c("scenario","sim_id")) |>
    dplyr::filter(is.na(bb_path) |
                  !(method %in% c("two_stage_bb","two_stage_bb_t"))) |>
    dplyr::select(-bb_path) |>
    dplyr::mutate(
      truth_use = ifelse(method == "joint_4pl",
                         .TRUTH_REL[as.character(quantity)],
                         truth),
      bias    = estimate - truth_use,
      covered = is.finite(lower) & is.finite(upper) &
                lower <= truth_use & truth_use <= upper
    ) |>
    dplyr::filter(success) |>
    dplyr::group_by(scenario, method, quantity) |>
    dplyr::summarise(
      n         = dplyr::n(),
      mean_bias = mean(bias, na.rm = TRUE),
      mcse_bias = stats::sd(bias, na.rm = TRUE) / sqrt(dplyr::n()),
      rmse      = sqrt(mean(bias^2, na.rm = TRUE)),
      coverage  = mean(covered, na.rm = TRUE),
      mcse_cov  = sqrt(mean(covered, na.rm = TRUE) *
                       (1 - mean(covered, na.rm = TRUE)) / dplyr::n()),
      .groups   = "drop"
    ) |>
    dplyr::mutate(
      method_code = as.character(method),
      method   = factor(method,   levels = .method_levels,
                                  labels = .method_labels),
      quantity = factor(quantity, levels = c("CTmax_1hr","z")),
      bias_lo  = mean_bias - 1.96 * mcse_bias,
      bias_hi  = mean_bias + 1.96 * mcse_bias,
      cov_lo   = coverage  - 1.96 * mcse_cov,
      cov_hi   = coverage  + 1.96 * mcse_cov
    )
}

# Count failed or implausible simulations by method and scenario.
.sim_fail_cache <- new.env(parent = emptyenv())
.sim_fail <- function(prefix, m) {
  if (is.null(.sim_fail_cache[[prefix]])) {
    files <- list.files(
      here::here("output", "sim_twostage"),
      pattern = paste0("^per_sim_", prefix, "_.*[.]rds$"), full.names = TRUE)
    .sim_fail_cache[[prefix]] <- dplyr::bind_rows(lapply(files, readRDS)) |>
      dplyr::group_by(scenario, method, sim_id) |>
      dplyr::summarise(bad = any(!success) |
                             any(quantity == "z"         & abs(bias) > 20) |
                             any(quantity == "CTmax_1hr" & abs(bias) > 10),
                       .groups = "drop") |>
      dplyr::group_by(scenario, method) |>
      dplyr::summarise(n = dplyr::n(), n_bad = sum(bad),
                       pct = 100 * mean(bad), .groups = "drop")
  }
  x <- dplyr::filter(.sim_fail_cache[[prefix]], method == m)
  if (nrow(x) == 0 || max(x$n_bad) == 0) return("0")
  lo <- min(x$n_bad); hi <- max(x$n_bad)
  plo <- min(x$pct);  phi <- max(x$pct)
  if (lo == hi) sprintf("%d (%.1f%%)", hi, phi)
  else sprintf("%d–%d (%.1f–%.1f%%)", lo, hi, plo, phi)
}

# Forest plot for cell-based scenarios (Scen 1, 2, 4, 8).
# `panel_var` is the column name to facet rows on (e.g. "panel_n" or "cell").
.plot_forest <- function(d, panel_var = "panel_n") {
  p_bias <- ggplot(d, aes(x = mean_bias, y = method, colour = method)) +
    geom_vline(xintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", colour = "grey50") +
    geom_errorbarh(aes(xmin = bias_lo, xmax = bias_hi), height = 0.25) +
    geom_point(size = 2.4) +
    facet_grid(reformulate("quantity", panel_var), scales = "free_x",
               labeller = labeller(quantity = .quantity_labeller)) +
    scale_colour_manual(values = .method_cols) +
    labs(x = "Mean bias (estimate - truth)", y = NULL,
         title = "a) Bias") +
    .theme_proj + theme(legend.position = "none")
  p_cov <- ggplot(d, aes(x = coverage, y = method, colour = method)) +
    geom_vline(xintercept = 0.95, linetype = "dashed", colour = "grey50") +
    geom_errorbarh(aes(xmin = cov_lo, xmax = cov_hi), height = 0.25) +
    geom_point(size = 2.4) +
    facet_grid(reformulate("quantity", panel_var), scales = "free_x",
               labeller = labeller(quantity = .quantity_labeller)) +
    scale_colour_manual(values = .method_cols) +
    scale_x_continuous(limits = c(0, 1), breaks = c(0, 0.5, 0.95)) +
    labs(x = "95% CI coverage", y = NULL,
         title = "b) Coverage") +
    .theme_proj + theme(legend.position = "none")
  p_bias / p_cov + plot_layout(heights = c(1, 1))
}

# Sweep coverage plot for Scen 6 / Scen 7.
.plot_sweep_cov <- function(d, sweep_x, x_label, x_breaks = waiver()) {
  ggplot(d, aes(x = .data[[sweep_x]], y = coverage, colour = method)) +
    geom_hline(yintercept = 0.95, linetype = "dashed", colour = "grey50") +
    geom_errorbar(aes(ymin = cov_lo, ymax = cov_hi), width = 0) +
    geom_point(size = 2.4) +
    geom_line() +
    facet_wrap(~ quantity, nrow = 1,
               labeller = labeller(quantity = .quantity_labeller)) +
    scale_colour_manual(values = .method_cols) +
    scale_x_continuous(breaks = x_breaks) +
    scale_y_continuous(limits = c(0, 1), breaks = c(0, 0.5, 0.95)) +
    labs(x = x_label, y = "95% CI coverage") +
    .theme_proj
}

# Build the RMSE table for z and CTmax.
.rmse_table <- function(d, cell_name = "Cell") {
  pe <- c(two_stage_bin = "Two-stage (binomial)",
          two_stage_bb  = "Two-stage (beta-binomial)",
          joint_4pl     = "Joint 4PL (relative)",
          joint_4pl_abs = "Joint 4PL (absolute)")
  out <- d |>
    dplyr::filter(method_code %in% names(pe)) |>
    dplyr::transmute(Cell, Method = factor(pe[method_code], levels = pe),
                     quantity = as.character(quantity), rmse = round(rmse, 2)) |>
    tidyr::pivot_wider(names_from = quantity, values_from = rmse) |>
    dplyr::arrange(Cell, Method) |>
    dplyr::select(Cell, Method, `z (°C)` = z, `CTmax (°C)` = CTmax_1hr)
  names(out)[1] <- cell_name
  # Escape underscores and other LaTeX-sensitive table text.
  tinytable::tt(out) |> tinytable::format_tt(escape = TRUE)
}
```

## *Strict equivalence baseline*

This scenario is the strict-equivalence baseline. The data-generating curve spans the full survival range ($\text{up} = 0.999$, $\text{low} = 0.001$), has a shared slope ($k = 8$), and has a midpoint that declines linearly with temperature (slope $-0.15$) under a binomial likelihood with no overdispersion. We crossed this scenario with two replicate designs, $n_{\text{reps}} \in \{3, 5\}$, with $N_{\text{sim}} = 1000$ datasets per cell.

We included this scenario to check whether the estimators agree when the classical two-stage assumptions are satisfied. As expected, all methods recovered $z$ and $CT_{max,1h}$ with little bias. The main difference was interval calibration: two-stage intervals based on Normal quantiles did not have nominal coverage, whereas the *t*-quantile delta-method intervals were close to the nominal 95% coverage. Again, it is important to recognise that we have ensured error from the two-stage is correctly propagated for comparison but often this is ignored.

Of the 1,000 datasets per cell, models in the two-stage analytical approach failed to fit or resulted in extreme bias — here and throughout, $|z|$ bias above 20 °C or $|CT_{max,1h}|$ bias above 10 °C, far outside any plausible value — for `r .sim_fail("scen1", "two_stage_bin")` binomial and `r .sim_fail("scen1", "two_stage_bb")` beta-binomial (glmmTMB) Stage-1 fits, versus `r .sim_fail("scen1", "joint_4pl")` for the joint 4PL, across the simulations. The overdispersion-corrected two-stage thus fails to converge on a sensible estimate even in this most benign scenario, whereas the joint 4PL does not.


```{r}
#| label: fig-sim-supp-scen1
#| echo: false
#| warning: false
#| message: false
#| fig-width: 8
#| fig-height: 8
#| fig-cap: "Bias and coverage at the strict-equivalence baseline (binomial DGP, asymptotes near 0/1, single slope $k$). Rows: $n_{reps}=3$ (top) and $n_{reps}=5$ (bottom). Columns: $z$ (left) and $CT_{max,1h}$ (right). Dashed reference lines at zero bias and nominal 0.95 coverage."

# Plot bias and coverage for simulation scenario 1.
.d <- .load_screened("scen1") |>
  dplyr::mutate(panel_n = paste0("n[reps] = ",
                                  sub(".*_n([0-9]+)$", "\\1", scenario)))
```
